quiz.title: Unit 6 Quiz 2: Money, Banking, and Financial Systems
quiz.subtitle: Monetary Policy, Commercial Banking, and Money Supply Measures
quiz.subject: Economics
quiz.topic: Money Supply, Commercial Banks, Central Banking, Credit Creation

questions.question: Giving permission to withdraw money by an amount more than deposited is known as _________
questions.explanation: Overdraft allows a customer to withdraw beyond their account balance up to an agreed limit.

questions.question: The narrow measure of money supply is __________
questions.explanation: M1 is the narrowest measure, consisting of currency in circulation and demand deposits.

questions.question: M3 money comprises the following EXCEPT ______________
questions.explanation: M3 includes M1 (currency, demand deposits), M2, plus large time deposits and institutional money market funds. Treasury bills are part of M4 (broader liquidity aggregate), not M3. This tests the distinction between M3 and M4 components.

questions.question: The overnight loans rate is defined as the interest rate on 24-hour loans that:
options: A. The Central Bank charges commercial banks for emergency liquidity.
B. Commercial banks charge their most creditworthy corporate borrowers.
C. Major commercial banks charge one another to manage daily reserve requirements.
D. Commercial banks pay to the Central Bank on excess settlement balances.
questions.explanation: The overnight rate (or federal funds rate in the U.S.) is an interbank rate. It is the interest rate at which major financial institutions lend and borrow funds among themselves for a one-day term. While the Central Bank sets a 'target' for this rate, the rate itself is determined by the interbank market.

questions.question: What is the primary motivation for a commercial bank to engage in the overnight interbank lending market?
options: To maintain mandatory reserve ratios and avoid central bank penalties
To secure low-cost capital for long-term mortgage disbursements
To increase the bank's statutory liquidity ratio in anticipation of a fiscal contraction
To hedge against long-term interest rate volatility in the bond market
questions.explanation: Commercial banks use the overnight market primarily to manage short-term liquidity fluctuations. By borrowing from banks with excess reserves, they ensure they meet the daily reserve requirements set by the central bank, thereby avoiding the higher interest rates typically charged at the discount window or other penalty facilities.

questions.question: The most liquid measure of money supply is ____________
questions.explanation: M1 contains the most liquid assets (currency and checking accounts).

questions.question: The Central bank is regarded as the lender of last resort. Which of the following is associated with this?
options: The price of central bank loan might be higher
The price of central bank loan might be lower
The central bank charges every bank unequally
The central bank reduces the reserve requirement for struggling banks
questions.explanation: Lender of last resort loans are typically at a penalty rate (higher than market) to discourage overuse. The central bank does not charge banks unequally as a matter of policy — discount window access is generally uniform. Reducing reserve requirements is not part of lender of last resort function.

questions.question: Which one of the following is an example of Bank of the Public?
questions.explanation: Bank of the Public refers to a commercial bank. Royal Bank of Canada is a commercial bank. The Federal Reserve and Bank of Canada are central banks.

questions.question: Which of the following is not a function of commercial banks?
options: Loan out funds to other banks
questions.explanation: Regulating the money supply is a function of the central bank, not commercial banks.

questions.question: If the central bank plans to have the interest rate settle at 4%, in a corridor system, they may ___________
options: Set the ceiling at 4.25% and floor at 3.75%
Set the ceiling at 4.25% and floor at 4%
Set the interest rate at exactly 4%
questions.explanation: In a corridor system, the central bank sets a lending rate (ceiling) and a deposit rate (floor). The market interest rate settles between them. A symmetric corridor around the target (e.g., 4% ± 0.25%) allows market forces to operate within bounds.

questions.question: If the central bank plans to have the interest rate settle at 4%, in a floor system, they may ___________
options: Set the ceiling at 4.25% and floor at 3.75%
Set the ceiling at 6% and floor at 4%
Set the interest rate at exactly 4%
questions.explanation: In a floor system, the central bank sets interest on excess reserves (IOER) as a floor below which market rates cannot fall. Setting the floor at the target rate (4%) ensures market rates settle at or slightly above 4%. The ceiling is set higher (6%) and is rarely binding. Option A describes a corridor system, not a floor system.

questions.question: _________ refers to the measures used by a central bank to regulate the amount of loans created by commercial banks in the economy.
questions.explanation: Credit control is the central bank's regulation of the volume and direction of credit (loans) created by commercial banks. Fiscal policy involves government spending and taxation. Loan policy and loan control are not standard macroeconomic terms.

questions.question: Banks with surplus reserves at night, when interest rate is high, ______________
options: hold idle money against high opportunity cost
hold idle money against low opportunity cost
can sell at low interest rate
questions.explanation: High interest rates mean high opportunity cost for holding idle reserves. Banks prefer to lend them out.

scoreMessages.100: Excellent! Strong grasp of banking and money supply concepts.
scoreMessages.80: Good work. Review the functions of commercial vs central banks.
scoreMessages.60: Solid effort. Focus on M1 vs broader money measures.
scoreMessages.0: Keep studying. M1 is narrow and most liquid.
